reforms.description: The federal government announced an additional C$1.3 billion programme for science and technology over the next five years, aimed at enhancing research and innovation capabilities.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The funding is intended to enhance research and innovation capabilities, which typically supports economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The programme was announced and is stated to be implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a significant but narrower funding initiative rather than a structural overhaul.
reforms.source_quote: The federal government announces an additional C$1.3 billion programme for science and technology over the next five years.

reforms.description: The Canadian government introduced a refundable R&D tax credit for small firms and broadened the definition of R&D for tax purposes to include immediate write-off of all current and capital expenditures.
reforms.component_name: R&D Tax Credit for Small Firms
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These tax incentives are designed to encourage private sector investment in R&D, which can lead to innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms were introduced and are stated to be implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of tax credits and broadening of definitions represents a significant change in the tax treatment of R&D.
reforms.source_quote: Definition broadened to include immediate write-off of all current and capital expenditures.

reforms.description: The Grandpre Commission proposed a doubling of the C$250 million spent on job training under the unemployment insurance programme, along with additional funding for existing programmes addressing skill shortages and a new training tax to promote private sector worker training.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Investing in job training and addressing skill shortages is expected to enhance the workforce's capabilities, supporting economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The proposal was made by the Grandpre Commission.
reforms.importance_rationale: The proposal represents a significant increase in funding for job training initiatives.
reforms.source_quote: However, it proposed a doubling of the C$250 million spent on job training under the unemployment insurance programme, C$300 million more for existing programmes dealing with skill shortages, a new training tax and tax concessions to promote private sector worker training.
